narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model estimated a 41.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we use to ensure we admit students likely to succeed. This prediction draws from patterns in data about past students' academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and enrollment choices, focusing on who passed the bar without protected factors like gender or race influencing the outcome. For you, the three biggest factors shaping this decision were your undergraduate GPA, family income level, and Year 3 grade decile in school. Your undergraduate GPA stood out as the strongest positive influence, as your 3.20 is close to the 3.24 average among those who passed, nudging the model toward seeing you as capable of success rather than failure. Similarly, your middle family income bracket aligned well with the majority of passers—about 35% came from similar backgrounds—giving another boost in the model's eyes toward predicting you'd pass. On the flip side, your Year 3 decile of 3 pulled in the opposite direction, since passers averaged around 5.91, making the model more concerned about potential struggles on the bar. Other elements, like your LSAT score of 30 being notably below the 37.27 average for passers and your Year 1 decile of 3 versus their 6.04 average, added to those worries but weren't the top drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have stronger early and later grade rankings alongside solid test scores, your profile showed promise in GPA and background but gaps in deciles that tipped the scales. Your choice of full-time enrollment matches the norm for passers at 93%, so that didn't hurt. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model highlighting risks based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the passing average, strengthening your grades to climb those deciles, and reapplying with an updated profile that addresses these key areas. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these improvements, and we're here to support your next steps.
